title: HTTP/3, QUIC, QPACK, Migration, and Production Protocol Defense · Execution Trace: Alt Svc Negotiation Fallback
summary: A release claims HTTP/3 because UDP is open, ignores QUIC connection identity and migration, treats QPACK like HPACK, advertises Alt-Svc without rollback, and cannot defend parser, TLS, proxy, or capacity evidence. Planned artifact: Add and verify the alt svc negotiation fallback increment in multi-version protocol release and incident defense. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Stage Alt-Svc discovery, certificate and authority checks, version negotiation, fallback, cache lifetime, disablement, and rollback without breaking HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2.
Explain QPACK encoder and decoder streams, dynamic-table capacity, blocked streams, acknowledgements, confidentiality, and decompression limits.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Alt Svc Negotiation Fallback · Theory Alt Svc Negotiation Fallback
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 1980 in a archival client parsing incomplete close-delimited responses. Build deterministic Go evidence to stage HTTP/3 through bounded QPACK, fallback health, and explicit rollback evidence; preserve this operating constraint: one connection has exactly one read owner and one terminal close owner; then saturate one stream window while another remains independently serviceable. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.
